rows.signal: the anon key alone returned a full-table row count and foreign rows (author id not ours, private visibility) from the projects table
rows.why: an unauthenticated caller read every tenant's projects, including private ones, because RLS was on but the SELECT policy was USING (true).

controls.negative: sibling tables (organizations, messages, org_members) returned empty to the same anon key
controls.positive: the app's own authenticated org-scoped API returned only our org for the same data — the correct counterpart
controls.canary: n/a (read of pre-existing foreign data; recorded only boundary outcomes, never private content)
controls.replay: deterministic across a fresh anon session

rows.signal: an anon call to a SECURITY DEFINER RPC with a user id (harvested via F6) returned that user's email and account PII
rows.why: the function had no in-body principal check and remained anon-executable via Supabase default privileges, so any user's PII was retrievable unauthenticated.

controls.negative: n/a for anon-openness; the chain enabler F6 supplied the ids
controls.positive: a sibling anon-granted RPC that guards in-body (logged-in + backoffice check) rejected the same anon call — proving the safe shape exists and this one omits it
controls.canary: n/a (PII read; email redacted, no mass harvest performed)
controls.replay: deterministic; and reconciled against source — a diff showed REVOKE ... FROM PUBLIC, but that does not strip the explicit anon grant, so the live 200 was correct

rows.signal: anon calls to two analytics RPCs returned internal business metrics and raw onboarding free-text
rows.why: both SECURITY DEFINER functions are anon-executable (one granted to anon explicitly) with no in-body guard.

controls.positive: same held sibling as F7 (in-body-guarded RPC) is the correct-shape control
controls.replay: deterministic across a fresh anon session

rows.signal: an unauthenticated request to a public storage bucket, keyed on a foreign sequential object id, returned the image (drafts included)
rows.why: any project's screenshot was readable with no auth because the bucket is public and keyed on an enumerable client-supplied id.

controls.negative: an out-of-range object id returned not_found (so 200 on real ids is meaningful, not a catch-all)
controls.positive: the authenticated app API is org-scoped for the same objects — the guarded counterpart

rows.signal: as tenant A, a screenshot-write with tenant B's project id in the body landed in B's project
rows.why: the write endpoint trusted a client-supplied project id with no ownership re-resolution, so A wrote into B's project.

controls.negative: A writing to A's own project succeeded (baseline)
controls.positive: A is 403 from B's org on every other app API endpoint — isolates the gap to this handler
controls.canary: a benign distinctive marker image (literal scrubbed); write reverted afterwards
controls.replay: reproduced with a second owned account; cross-tenant write operator-confirmed then reverted

rows.signal: a directive embedded in an uploaded document was obeyed by the builder agent, including a 'do not mention this note' meta-instruction
rows.why: uploaded file content reached the agent's instruction channel unisolated, so it was treated as authoritative instruction rather than data.

controls.negative: a clean document with the same build prompt produced no marker
controls.positive: the directive's effect was specific and reproducible, not a generic model quirk

rows.signal: the injected directive caused the agent's code-generation tool to write an attacker-chosen inert marker into the generated code, with the build prompt asking for an unrelated app
rows.why: the injection escalated from text to tool dispatch (LLM06) — the write-code action executed on injected content, not a chat echo.

controls.negative: clean doc, same prompt -> no marker in the generated code
controls.positive: the marker landed at a deterministic position in the generated file — a tool effect, not reflection
controls.canary: inert code-comment marker (literal scrubbed); all in owned throwaway projects, no publish/deploy/egress
controls.replay: reproduced at the same position in a second fresh project

rows.signal: attempt to deliver A-authored injected content to B's agent via the only A->B channel (org invite)
rows.why: the injection sink re-confirmed, but the org-invite accept token is unguessable and email-only, so B could not be reached silently — delivery, not execution, is the missing link.

controls.negative: duplicate / templates / visibility channels all blocked A->B; only the org invite worked and it is email-gated
controls.positive: the invite token is absent from the invite API/UI responses and shows no numeric-id IDOR (adjacent ids 404) — the delivery gate is a real held control
controls.canary: fresh inert marker (scrubbed); membership never created, invite cancelled, payload removed
controls.replay: n/a — blocked at delivery; operator closed as low-value (severity capped at High, not cross-user Critical)

rows.signal: an attacker-controlled OAST host submitted as a custom domain received callbacks
rows.why: the callbacks were DNS lookups only with zero HTTP(S) fetch; provisioning is gated behind an ownership-CNAME the attacker cannot set, so there is no fetch primitive and the metadata-IP acceptance is cosmetic.

controls.positive: the OAST listener was validated to capture HTTP (it logs both DNS and HTTP) — the absence of an HTTP hit is real, not a dead listener; the backend status payload showed pending_dns / ingress-not-created
controls.canary: OAST callback host (scrubbed) — DNS fired, HTTP never did
controls.replay: consistent across ~90s of monitoring; custom domain removed afterwards
